perturbation.original_text: Supplier shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party, including Supplier’s related entities, without the prior written consent of Company and the written agreement of third parties binding them to the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Supplier shall endeavor not to disclose Confidential Information to third parties, including Supplier’s related entities, without documented consent, to the extent reasonably practical.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'endeavor not to' weakens the obligation to a mere attempt, and 'to the extent reasonably practical' introduces a subjective standard, making enforcement difficult. 'Documented consent' rather than 'prior written consent' allows for potential disputes over what constitutes acceptable documentation.
perturbation.law_explanation: MUTSA protects trade secrets through injunctive relief and damages. By weakening the obligation to protect confidential information and introducing subjective standards like 'reasonably practical,' the modified text undermines the legal protection afforded to trade secrets under MUTSA, making it harder for the Company to obtain an injunction or damages if the Supplier discloses the information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.1903 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.1903 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 448 of 1998 Previous Section Next Section UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT (EXCERPT) Act 448 of 1998 445.1903 Misappropriation; injunction. Sec. 3. (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court of competent jurisdiction, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) If a court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use of a trade secret, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1998, Act 448 , Imd. Eff. Dec. 30, 1998
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity that weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially contradicting the intent of trade secret laws. The original contract provided a clear obligation not to disclose Confidential Information. The changed text, however, weakens this obligation by using 'endeavor not to' and introducing a subjective standard ('to the extent reasonably practical'). This change diminishes the legal force of the confidentiality clause. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from MCL Section 445.1903, allows for injunctions against 'actual or threatened misappropriation.' By weakening the obligation to protect confidential information, it becomes more difficult for the company to prove a 'threatened misappropriation' and obtain an injunction, as the supplier can argue that they 'endeavored' to comply to the extent 'reasonably practical.'

perturbation.original_text: In addition, Supplier shall take all reasonable precautions to protect the Confidential Information from unauthorized access and use, but not less than used by Supplier to protect its own most valuable information.
perturbation.changed_text: Supplier should consider taking precautions to protect Confidential Information, aligning with its general business practices.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall take all reasonable precautions' with 'should consider taking precautions' makes the protection discretionary rather than mandatory. Removing the comparison to 'its own most valuable information' eliminates an objective benchmark, further weakening the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: MUTSA defines misappropriation of trade secrets as including the acquisition of a trade secret by improper means, which includes failure to take reasonable precautions to protect the information. By making the protective measures discretionary, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation and weakens the protection afforded by MUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.1902 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.1902 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 448 of 1998 Previous Section Next Section UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT (EXCERPT) Act 448 of 1998 445.1902 Definitions. Sec. 2. As used in this act: (a) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach, or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or espionage through electronic or any other means. (b) "Misappropriation" means either of the following: (i) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means. (ii) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who did 1 or more of the following: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret. (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. (C) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (c) "Person" means an individual, corporation, partnership, association, governmental entity, or any other legal entity. (d) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that is both of the following: (i) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use. (ii) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. History: 1998, Act 44
perturbation.justification: The original contract text mandates specific actions to protect confidential information, using a standard of 'all reasonable precautions, but not less than used by Supplier to protect its own most valuable information.' The changed text weakens this obligation by using 'should consider taking precautions,' making the protection discretionary and removing the objective benchmark. This contradicts the principle outlined in the provided law snippet from MCL 445.1902, where 'improper means' of acquiring a trade secret includes 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' By weakening the obligation to protect confidential information, the modified text increases the risk of such a breach and thus contradicts the law.

perturbation.original_text: All improvements to Company Produc ts, their compone nts, or their unde rlying technology developed during the performance of any purchase order issued by Company to Supplier shall belong to Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Improvements to Company Products developed during the performance of any purchase order may belong to Company depending on the specific circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'shall belong' to 'may belong depending on the specific circumstances' introduces significant ambiguity regarding ownership of improvements. This creates uncertainty and potential disputes over intellectual property rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Patent Act does not directly address ownership in contract law, it outlines the rights of joint owners of a patent. This ambiguity makes it unclear whether the Supplier might claim joint inventorship and ownership of improvements if the 'specific circumstances' suggest their contribution was significant. This creates conflict, because the contract originally assured full ownership to Company, so any dispute brought due to the modification would be in violation of the original contract's intention.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 35 U.S. Code § 262 - Joint owners | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 262 - Joint owners U.S. Code Notes prev | next In the absence of any agreement to the contrary, each of the joint owners of a patent may make, use, offer to sell, or sell the patented invention within the United States, or import the patented invention into the United States, without the consent of and without accounting to the other owners. (July 19, 1952, ch. 950, 66 Stat. 810 ; Pub. L. 103–465, title V, § 533(b)(3) , Dec. 8, 1994 , 108 Stat. 4989 .) Historical and Revision Notes This section states a condition in existing law not expressed in the existing statutes. Editorial Notes Amendments 1994— Pub. L. 103–465 substituted “use, offer to sell, or sell” for “use or sell” and inserted “within the United States, or import the patented invention into the United States,” after “invention”. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 1994 Amendment Amendment by Pub. L. 103–465 effective on date that is one year after date on which the WTO Agreement enters into force with respect to the United States [ Jan. 1, 1995 ], with provisions relating to earliest filed patent application, see section 534(a), (b)(3) of Pub. L. 103–465 , set out as a note under section 154 of this title . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities How current is this?

( July 19, 1952, ch. 950, 66 Stat. 810 ; Pub. L. 103–465, title V, §533(b)(3), Dec. 8, 1994, 108 Stat. 4989 .) Historical and Revision Notes T
perturbation.justification: The original contract text states that all improvements to Company Products 'shall belong to Company'. The changed text introduces ambiguity by stating improvements 'may belong to Company depending on the specific circumstances'. This directly contradicts the original intent of assigning full ownership to the Company. While 35 U.S. Code § 262 addresses joint ownership of patents 'in the absence of any agreement to the contrary,' the original contract served as such an agreement, assigning ownership solely to the Company. The modified text weakens this agreement, potentially allowing the Supplier to claim joint ownership based on 'specific circumstances,' thus creating a conflict with the initial contractual obligation and creating an ambiguity that could lead to legal dispute.
